claims.claim_text: The module presents a specific 2019 Uber payment-feature incident as a real historical event.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: In 2019, engineering teams at Uber were preparing to launch a highly anticipated payment feature.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Teams shipping payment code routinely rely on code review and test coverage, but subtle edge cases can still slip through before release.
claims.rationale: I could not find an authoritative source for this named Uber anecdote, so the named event should be removed.

claims.claim_text: The module says Uber used an early AI code analyzer that caught the bug before deployment.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: However, before deployment, they ran the codebase through an early AI code analyzer to act as a final safety net.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Additional automated analysis can sometimes catch input-validation bugs that standard review misses before release.
claims.rationale: This is part of the same unattributed incident narrative and should be rewritten without false specifics.

claims.claim_text: The module assigns the Uber anecdote a quantified exposure of more than $500,000.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: The financial impact of this single oversight was estimated at over $500,000 in potential exposure had it reached production.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A missing validation check in payment logic can create serious fraud exposure if it reaches production.
claims.rationale: The precise dollar figure is unsupported and should not remain in a historical anecdote without a primary source.

claims.claim_text: The module mixes GitHub Copilot Free launch facts with later 'premium requests' terminology.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Free Tier Explosion
claims.anchor_text: Shortly after, the GitHub Copilot Free plan was announced on December 18, 2024, requiring only a GitHub account and providing up to 2,000 inline code completions and 50 premium requests per month.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Shortly after, GitHub announced Copilot Free on December 18, 2024. At launch, anyone with a personal GitHub account could use 2,000 code completions and 50 chat messages per month.
claims.rationale: GitHub's launch post supports the date, personal-account access, and 2,000 completions plus 50 chat messages, but not the later 'premium requests' phrasing for that launch sentence.

claims.claim_text: GitHub Copilot Pro costs $10 per month and Copilot Pro+ costs $39 per month.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Free Tier Explosion
claims.rationale: GitHub's plans page directly lists Pro at $10/month and Pro+ at $39/month.

claims.claim_text: Amazon CodeWhisperer became part of Amazon Q Developer on April 30, 2024.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Free Tier Explosion
claims.rationale: AWS documentation directly states that Amazon CodeWhisperer became part of Amazon Q Developer on April 30, 2024.

claims.claim_text: Amazon Q Developer offers a free tier with 50 agentic requests and 1,000 lines of Java transformation per month, while Pro costs $19/month.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Free Tier Explosion
claims.rationale: AWS pricing documentation directly lists 50 free agentic requests, 1,000 free Java-upgrade LOC per month, and a $19/month Pro tier.

claims.claim_text: Google offers a free Gemini Code Assist tier for individual developers.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Free Tier Explosion
claims.rationale: Google Cloud documentation explicitly refers readers to 'Gemini Code Assist for individuals, the free version of Gemini Code Assist.'

claims.claim_text: The module cites an unverified '180,000 code completions per month' figure for Gemini Code Assist.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Free Tier Explosion
claims.anchor_text: However, secondary claims stating Gemini provides up to 180,000 code completions per month remain unverified, as official documentation obscures the exact quotas.
claims.suggested_rewrite: However, public secondary reports about Gemini Code Assist quotas have varied, and Google's official documentation does not clearly publish a simple monthly completion number here.
claims.rationale: The exact 180,000 figure is not supported by the official documentation I found and should not be presented as a concrete number.

claims.claim_text: The module gives an incorrect exact removal date for codex-mini-latest.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Evolution of OpenAI Codex
claims.anchor_text: Concurrently, the legacy `codex-mini-latest` model was fully removed from the OpenAI API on February 12, 2026.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Later, the legacy `codex-mini-latest` model was deprecated and removed from the OpenAI API.
claims.rationale: OpenAI's deprecations page shows a different removal date, so the exact date in the module should be removed unless corrected from the primary source.

claims.claim_text: Codestral 25.01 launched on January 13, 2025, with a 256K context window, an 86.6% HumanEval score, and roughly 2x faster generation than its predecessor.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Open-Source MoE Surge
claims.rationale: Mistral's January 13, 2025 announcement directly provides the date, benchmark table, 256K context length, and 2x speed claim.

claims.claim_text: Codestral 25.08 improved accepted completions by 30% over prior versions.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Open-Source MoE Surge
claims.rationale: Mistral's Codestral 25.08 announcement explicitly states a +30% increase in accepted completions.

claims.claim_text: Using raw user-controlled file paths can lead to path traversal attacks.
claims.span_hint: section: Security Considerations > Input Validation
claims.rationale: OWASP's Path Traversal page directly explains that manipulating file-path inputs can access unauthorized files and directories.

claims.claim_text: Parameterized queries are a preferred defense against SQL injection.
claims.span_hint: section: Security Considerations > SQL Injection Prevention
claims.rationale: OWASP's Injection guidance explicitly says the preferred defense is keeping data separate from commands and queries via safe or parameterized interfaces.

claims.claim_text: Constructing shell commands from unsafe user input can lead to command injection, and structured argument passing is safer.
claims.span_hint: section: Security Considerations > Command Injection Prevention
claims.rationale: OWASP's OS Command Injection cheat sheet directly recommends avoiding shell command construction from user input and using structured mechanisms plus validation.

claims.claim_text: The module overstates legal certainty by saying deployers automatically assume all IP liability for generated code.
claims.span_hint: section: Legal and Ethical Constraints
claims.anchor_text: If you deploy proprietary systems using generated code, you assume all liability for intellectual property violations.
claims.suggested_rewrite: If you deploy generated code in proprietary systems, you still need legal review for licensing and intellectual-property risk.
claims.rationale: This is an overbroad legal conclusion that should be reframed as a risk-management point rather than a categorical statement.

claims.claim_text: The module overgeneralizes by claiming that many enterprise AI tools have duplicate-detection features that warn on exact public-code matches.
claims.span_hint: section: Legal and Ethical Constraints
claims.anchor_text: Many enterprise AI tools now feature duplicate detection to warn developers if an output exactly matches a public GPL-licensed repository.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Some AI coding tools can surface references when generated code matches public repositories, helping developers review licensing risk.
claims.rationale: I found direct support for GitHub Copilot code references, but not for the broader 'many enterprise AI tools' generalization as written.

claims.claim_text: The module gives a highly specific Google-Windsurf-OpenAI deal narrative with dollar figures and dates.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The $2.4 Billion IDE Bidding War
claims.anchor_text: Google acquired the leadership of Windsurf (formerly Codeium) in a $2.4 billion deal announced on July 11, 2025, moving in after OpenAI's $3 billion acquisition attempt collapsed over IP rights.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The market for AI-native editors has seen high-profile deal activity, failed transactions, and leadership reshuffles.
claims.rationale: This sentence bundles several precise corporate-event claims that need primary sourcing and should not remain as-is without it.

claims.claim_text: The module reports specific contested details about Windsurf's remaining assets and acquisition reporting.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The $2.4 Billion IDE Bidding War
claims.anchor_text: The fate of Windsurf's remaining assets has yielded conflicting reports: authoritative tech outlets state Cognition AI acquired the assets on July 14, 2025, while some secondary reviews incorrectly date the acquisition to December 2025 for a mere $250 million.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Reporting around Windsurf's remaining assets was inconsistent, so avoid teaching with precise figures unless you can cite the primary announcement.
claims.rationale: The sentence is explicitly about conflicting reporting and should not preserve exact figures and dates without a primary announcement.

claims.claim_text: Cursor changed its June 2025 individual Pro pricing to a usage-credit model that included $20 of frontier model usage per month.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The $2.4 Billion IDE Bidding War
claims.rationale: Cursor's own pricing post supports this claim, but the domain is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: JetBrains documentation published on March 30, 2026 describes installing agents from a curated ACP registry inside JetBrains IDEs.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The $2.4 Billion IDE Bidding War
claims.rationale: JetBrains' own documentation supports the registry-install flow, but jetbrains.com is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Tabnine advertises deployment options including fully air-gapped environments.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The $2.4 Billion IDE Bidding War
claims.rationale: Tabnine's platform page directly claims SaaS, on-prem, and fully air-gapped deployment, but the domain is off the current allowlist.

further_reading.title: OWASP Top 10: Injection
further_reading.why_relevant: Grounds the module's security-review advice in a standard reference on injection risks and defenses.

notes: No shared section pool was available in this run, so all supported claims use direct proposed URLs. Off-allowlist vendor/editor claims were marked needs_allowlist_expansion. The Uber and Windsurf anecdotes were treated conservatively because I could not verify their specific dates, dollar figures, and event framing from allowlisted primary sources.
